destination.name: Jerez & Sherry
destination.hero_image_credit: Photo by El Pantera on Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 4.0)
destination.hero_image_alt: Palomino Fino vineyards on bright white albariza chalk soil in Jerez de la Frontera, Andalusia
destination.blurb: Home of sherry, the fortified-wine category that spans biologically aged Fino and Manzanilla through oxidative Amontillado, Oloroso and Palo Cortado. From chalky albariza vineyards surrounding three historic towns, Palomino Fino grapes and the flor yeast film produce these styles under two protected designations: DO Jerez-Xérès-Sherry and DO Manzanilla-Sanlúcar de Barrameda.
destination.tagline: Two DOs, three towns, one chalky terroir, flor yeast
destination.overview: The Sherry Triangle in southern Andalusia is defined by Jerez de la Frontera, Sanlúcar de Barrameda and El Puerto de Santa María. Albariza chalk soil retains winter rain through dry summers. DO Jerez-Xérès-Sherry covers all styles; DO Manzanilla-Sanlúcar de Barrameda covers only Manzanilla from Sanlúcar's Atlantic-cooled cellars.

index.title: Jerez & Sherry Wine Region Guide | Cork & Curve
index.description: Explore the Sherry Triangle: DO Jerez and DO Manzanilla-Sanlúcar de Barrameda. Bodegas, VORS tastings, albariza vineyards and the solera system explained.

vineyards.description: Discover the leading bodegas of the Sherry Triangle: González Byass, Lustau, Barbadillo, Delgado Zuleta, Valdespino, El Maestro Sierra and 45 more producers.

wines.title: Sherry Wines Catalog | Cork & Curve
wines.description: Browse sherry cuvées by style: Fino, Manzanilla, Amontillado, Palo Cortado, Oloroso, Pedro Ximénez and VORS. Taste profiles, pairings and solera histories.

tasting-rooms.description: The best sherry tasting experiences in Jerez de la Frontera, Sanlúcar de Barrameda and El Puerto de Santa María. Bodega tours and cellar-door flights.

wine-bars.title: Sherry Bars in Jerez and Sanlúcar | Cork & Curve
wine-bars.description: Sommelier-led sherry bars and tabancas pouring Fino, Manzanilla En Rama, Palo Cortado and VORS Oloroso across Jerez, Sanlúcar and El Puerto de Santa María.

wine-restaurants.title: Jerez Wine Restaurants | Cork & Curve
wine-restaurants.description: Restaurants in the Sherry Triangle with serious sherry programs: Fino with fried seafood, Oloroso with game and the classic Andalusian pairings of Cádiz province.

signature-wines.title: Iconic Sherry Wines | Cork & Curve
signature-wines.description: Iconic sherries of Jerez: Tío Pepe Fino, La Gitana Manzanilla, Lustau Almacenista, Valdespino Inocente, Bodegas Tradición VORS, El Maestro Sierra VORS and more.

signature-grapes.title: Jerez Signature Grapes | Cork & Curve
signature-grapes.description: Palomino Fino, Pedro Ximénez and Moscatel de Alejandría: the three grape varieties authorised in DO Jerez-Xérès-Sherry and their roles in sherry production.

neighborhoods.title: Jerez Sub-Appellations and Pagos | Cork & Curve
neighborhoods.description: The Sherry Triangle towns and single-vineyard pagos: Macharnudo, Carrascal, Balbaina, Añina, Miraflores and the three bodega towns of Jerez, Sanlúcar and El Puerto.

faqs.question: What is the difference between DO Jerez-Xérès-Sherry and DO Manzanilla-Sanlúcar de Barrameda?
faqs.answer: DO Jerez-Xérès-Sherry governs all sherry styles produced anywhere in the Sherry Triangle (Jerez de la Frontera, Sanlúcar de Barrameda and El Puerto de Santa María). DO Manzanilla-Sanlúcar de Barrameda is a geographically nested DO that applies exclusively to Manzanilla aged in Sanlúcar. A Manzanilla from Sanlúcar can carry both DO labels; no other sherry style can use the Manzanilla-Sanlúcar DO.

faqs.question: What is albariza soil and why does it matter for sherry?
faqs.answer: Albariza is the brilliantly white chalky soil that covers roughly 95 percent of the premium sherry vineyards. It is rich in calcium carbonate, which reflects sunlight onto the vine canopy and, critically, absorbs winter rain into a dense, moisture-retaining sponge that sustains Palomino Fino through Andalusia's dry, hot summers without irrigation.

faqs.question: What is flor and how does it determine sherry style?
faqs.answer: Flor is a film of naturally occurring yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) that forms on the surface of wine in the barrel if alcohol is kept at or below 15 percent after fortification. Flor protects the wine from oxygen and gives biological-style sherries (Fino and Manzanilla) their characteristic pale colour, sharp acidity and saline-almond character. If flor dies or is prevented, the wine ages oxidatively to become Oloroso. Amontillado begins as Fino under flor and transitions to oxidative ageing; Palo Cortado is an oxidative style that retains aromatic finesse associated with biological wines.

faqs.question: What do VOS and VORS mean on a sherry label?
faqs.answer: VOS (Very Old Sherry) certifies an average solera age of at least 20 years; VORS (Very Old Rare Sherry) certifies at least 30 years. Both require carbon-dating verification by the Consejo Regulador de las Denominaciones de Origen Jerez-Xérès-Sherry. Only Amontillado, Oloroso, Palo Cortado and Pedro Ximénez can carry VOS or VORS certification.

faqs.question: What is En Rama sherry?
faqs.answer: En Rama is a bottling designation for Fino and Manzanilla drawn with minimal clarification and filtration directly from the solera. It preserves the full flor character, texture and complexity of the wine as it exists in cask. En Rama releases are seasonal and bottle-variable; they are typically released in spring when flor is most active.

faqs.question: What are the three towns of the Sherry Triangle?
faqs.answer: Jerez de la Frontera is the largest and most historic bodega town. Sanlúcar de Barrameda at the Guadalquivir estuary mouth produces exclusively Manzanilla under its own DO. El Puerto de Santa María is the historic shipping port, where Atlantic winds condition Palomino differently again, giving a slightly fuller, less saline character than Sanlúcar.

faqs.question: What food pairs well with Fino and Manzanilla?
faqs.answer: Both styles are dry, high-acid and saline: natural partners for jamón ibérico, fried anchovies, gambas al ajillo, fresh shellfish, sushi, olives and any fried food. The bone-dry finish and flor-driven nuttiness also work with salted almonds and tempura. Fino from Jerez is slightly rounder; Manzanilla from Sanlúcar is leaner and saltier.

faqs.question: How should sherry be stored and served?
faqs.answer: Fino and Manzanilla should be refrigerated and drunk within a few weeks of purchase; they are live wines that continue to evolve after bottling. Amontillado and Palo Cortado are best at 12 to 14 degrees Celsius. Oloroso and Pedro Ximénez can be served at room temperature or lightly chilled. VORS wines benefit from decanting briefly before serving. Unlike table wine, an open bottle of Oloroso or PX remains stable for weeks or months.
